description: Common rework causes, troubleshooting, trade coordination needs, and field calculations for pipe fitting and industrial erection

ranked_by_frequency.details: Installing A106 Gr B where A335 P11 is specified. Mixing 304 and 316 components. Wrong flange rating. Wrong gasket material.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: PMI all alloy materials. Verify marking against MTR. Color code piping materials in laydown. Double-check spec class on ISO.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: High — entire spool may need replacement. If found after welding, even worse.

ranked_by_frequency.details: Porosity (wet rods), lack of fusion (low heat), incomplete penetration (bad fit-up), cracks (hydrogen, no preheat).
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: Rod oven discipline. Proper fit-up and root gap. Preheat compliance. Qualified welders.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: Medium to high — weld repair is 3-5x the cost of original weld. Multiple repairs may require cut-out and re-weld.

ranked_by_frequency.issue: Dimensional errors — fabricated to wrong dimensions
ranked_by_frequency.details: Spool too long, too short, wrong angle on miter, wrong orientation. Fabricating from wrong revision drawing.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: Verify drawing revision. Measure twice, cut once. Account for weld shrinkage (approximately 1/16 to 1/8 inch per butt weld). Use field fit pieces.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: Medium to high — may require re-fabrication of entire spool.

ranked_by_frequency.details: Bolt holes not straddling centerline (vertical or horizontal per spec). Discovered after spool is welded and NDE'd.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: Mark bolt hole orientation BEFORE welding. Check spec — most require bolt holes to straddle centerline (not sit on it).
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: Medium — requires cutting flange off and re-welding in correct orientation.

ranked_by_frequency.issue: Fit-up out of tolerance — hi-lo, gap, alignment
ranked_by_frequency.details: Internal misalignment exceeding 1/16 inch. Root gap outside WPS range. Pipe not aligned to mating equipment nozzle.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: Use proper fit-up clamps. Measure with gauges (not eyeball). Check tolerances before tacking.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: Low if caught before welding (re-fit). High if caught after welding and NDE (cut out and re-do).

ranked_by_frequency.issue: PWHT not performed or improperly performed
ranked_by_frequency.details: Forgot PWHT on chrome-moly weld. PWHT temperature too low/high. Heating rate exceeded. Found at hardness testing.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: PWHT tracking system. Thermocouple monitoring and recording. Hardness testing after PWHT.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: High — may require re-PWHT (costly mobilization) or complete re-weld if microstructure damaged.

ranked_by_frequency.details: Preheat dropped below minimum during welding (torch preheat burned out, blankets failed, wind). Causes hydrogen cracking.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: Continuous temperature monitoring. Use electric resistance heating pads with controllers. Wind screens.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: High if cracking occurs — may not be detected until NDE days later. Full cut-out and re-weld.

ranked_by_frequency.issue: Pipe supports missing, wrong, or in wrong location
ranked_by_frequency.details: Pipe installed without proper supports. Supports at wrong elevation. Missing spring hangers. Wrong spring range.
ranked_by_frequency.prevention: Install supports BEFORE pipe runs. Verify support drawing against pipe ISO. Check spring hanger tags against schedule.
ranked_by_frequency.cost_impact: Low to medium — but can delay hydro test and commissioning significantly.

common_causes.diagnosis: Check torque with calibrated wrench. Look for uneven gap between flanges (wider on one side).
common_causes.fix: Re-torque using proper star pattern, 4-pass sequence. May need to break joint and start over with new gasket.

common_causes.diagnosis: Verify gasket material and type match piping spec class. Check gasket rating vs operating conditions.
common_causes.fix: Replace with correct gasket. NEVER reuse gaskets.

common_causes.diagnosis: Inspect for scratches, gouges, corrosion across serrations. Use straightedge to check for warpage.
common_causes.fix: Machine flange face if damage is minor. Replace flange if warped or deeply gouged.

common_causes.diagnosis: Measure gap at 4 points 90 degrees apart. Variation exceeding 1/16 inch per foot of OD indicates problem.
common_causes.fix: Re-align pipe and flanges. Do NOT use bolts to pull flanges together — this spring-loads the joint.

common_causes.diagnosis: Re-check torque 4-24 hours after initial bolt-up. Gasket embedment causes initial relaxation.
common_causes.fix: Re-torque to target value. Some specs require a second re-torque after 24 hours.

common_causes.cause: Thermal cycling — bolts expand differently than flanges
common_causes.diagnosis: Leak appears after system heats up or cools down. Common with B7 bolts on stainless flanges.
common_causes.fix: Use live-loading (Belleville washers) on flanges subject to thermal cycling. Re-torque at operating temperature.

common_causes.diagnosis: Gasket protruding on one side or visible off-center through inspection port.
Bolts show galling (stainless), or torque wrench clicks but bolt tension is low (dry bolt gets ~50% less clamp load).
common_causes.fix: Use specified anti-seize/lubricant. Replace galled bolts.

common_problems.cause: Supports too far apart, support elevation wrong, spring hanger not carrying load
common_problems.effect: Low spots collect condensate (water hammer in steam, corrosion in any service), stress on welds
common_problems.fix: Add intermediate support, re-level existing supports, check spring hanger load

common_problems.cause: Flow-induced vibration, mechanical vibration from equipment, unsupported small-bore connections
common_problems.effect: Fatigue cracking at welds and branch connections. Socket weld branch connections most vulnerable.
common_problems.fix: Add supports or braces at vibration nodes. Stiffen small-bore connections. Add snubbers for dynamic loads.

common_problems.issue: Spring hanger topped out or bottomed out
common_problems.cause: Wrong spring range selected, unexpected thermal growth, support elevation wrong
common_problems.effect: Pipe carries full dead weight (spring topped out) or spring overloaded (bottomed out). Nozzle loads on equipment exceeded.
common_problems.fix: Verify spring against hanger schedule. Re-set or replace with correct spring range. Check pipe elevation and thermal growth.

common_problems.cause: Thermal growth lifting pipe at support location. Anchor point arrangement causing uplift.
common_problems.effect: Pipe unsupported — overloads adjacent supports. Noise from pipe banging down on support.
common_problems.fix: Add hold-down clamp or change support type to guided/restrained. Engineering review of flexibility analysis.

common_problems.issue: Excessive pipe expansion damaging pipe or equipment
common_problems.cause: No expansion loops or expansion joints, anchors in wrong location, guides too tight
common_problems.effect: Equipment nozzle overload, cracked welds, displaced pipe
common_problems.fix: Engineering review of pipe flexibility. Add expansion loops, expansion joints, or re-route pipe. Adjust guide clearances.

pipe_to_equipment_nozzle.problem: Pipe doesn't line up with equipment nozzle after fabrication
causes: Equipment nozzle location different from drawing (tolerance stack-up)
Thermal growth not accounted for in cold position
solutions: Use field-fit closure spools — leave one joint per system as field-measured/cut
Adjust pipe supports to bring pipe into alignment
Cold spring per engineering approval ONLY (intentional misalignment in cold that aligns at operating temp)
NEVER force pipe into position — this loads equipment nozzles and causes failure

pipe_to_pipe.problem: Mating spools don't align — offset, rotated, or wrong length
solutions: If offset small (<1/4 inch), may be possible to absorb in flange gasket (engineering approval needed)
Check for cumulative tolerance stack-up — many small errors add up on long runs

structural_steel_alignment.problem: Steel members not plumb, level, or dimensionally correct
structural_steel_alignment.impact: Pipe supports, equipment foundations, and platform elevations all depend on steel being right
solutions: Survey check steel against drawings before piping starts
Shim pipe supports to correct for steel out-of-tolerance
Report significant deviations to engineering for disposition

civil: Equipment foundations complete, surveyed, and accepted — pipe cannot connect to equipment that isn't set
Pipe support foundations/embed plates installed at correct elevation and location
Underground piping sleeves and penetrations cast in foundations before concrete pour
Fireproofing complete on structural steel before piping starts (pipe blocks fireproofing access)
structural_steel: Pipe rack steel erected and bolted/welded — pipe cannot be set on incomplete racks
Platforms and access steel complete for elevated pipe work
Pipe support steel (clip angles, beam clamps, dummy leg supports) installed
Column and beam fireproofing complete before pipe runs (access issue)
electrical: Heat trace circuits designed and cables available for installation with piping
Power available for welding machines at work locations
Temporary lighting at elevated work areas
Electrical heat trace installed AFTER hydro test but BEFORE insulation
instrumentation: Instrument connection details (thermowell lengths, orifice flange tap locations, control valve face-to-face dimensions)
Instrument air available for pneumatic testing
Control valve bodies delivered for in-line installation (pipe fitters install the valve body, instrument techs install actuator/positioner)
insulation: All piping hydro tested and accepted BEFORE insulation
Steam tracing installed and tested BEFORE insulation
All pipe supports correct (insulation goes over shoes)

instrument_connections: Process taps (nipples, valves, orifice flanges) installed and hydro tested before instrument installation
Thermowells welded into pipe during fabrication — cannot add after hydro
Control valve bodies in line and flanged connections made up
electrical: Conduit supports on pipe racks — electricians need pipe rack steel AND pipe fitters to provide support locations
Grounding connections on piping systems where specified
Clearance for cable tray routing — pipe fitters need to maintain routing corridors per 3D model
structural: Pipe loads provided to structural engineer for pipe support design
Hot pipe expansion data for support/guide design
Location of heavy valves for dedicated support steel
civil: Underground pipe locations and elevations for civil excavation
Pipe sleeve sizes for foundation penetrations
Anchor bolt patterns for pipe support foundations

coordination_meetings.daily_foreman_meeting: All trade foremen meet daily to discuss next-day work, identify conflicts, request support
coordination_meetings.weekly_area_coordination: Area coordinator leads meeting to review 3-week look-ahead, sequence work, resolve interferences
coordination_meetings.interference_resolution: When trades conflict for same space: process piping typically has priority, but all interferences require engineering disposition

hydro_test_pressure.formula: Test Pressure = 1.5 x Design Pressure (per ASME B31.3 for standard hydro test)
examples: 150# system designed for 285 psi: Test pressure = 285 x 1.5 = 427.5 psi
300# system designed for 740 psi: Test pressure = 740 x 1.5 = 1110 psi
600# system designed for 1480 psi: Test pressure = 1480 x 1.5 = 2220 psi
hydro_test_pressure.temperature_correction: If test temperature is higher than design temperature (unusual), multiply by stress ratio: Pt = 1.5 x P x (St_at_test / St_at_design). Usually test temp is lower, so this increases test pressure slightly.
hydro_test_pressure.note: Test pressure must not exceed maximum allowable pressure of weakest component in test boundary (flanges, valves, vessels connected to test system)

pneumatic_test_pressure.formula: Test Pressure = 1.1 x Design Pressure (per ASME B31.3 paragraph 345.5)
examples: System designed for 285 psi: Pneumatic test = 285 x 1.1 = 313.5 psi
System designed for 740 psi: Pneumatic test = 740 x 1.1 = 814 psi
pneumatic_test_pressure.note: Pneumatic test pressure is LOWER than hydro because stored energy is much higher with gas. Still extremely dangerous.

bolt_torque_calculation.simplified_formula: T = K x D x F

where.K: Nut factor (dimensionless) — accounts for friction. K = 0.20 for lubricated steel, 0.15 for moly-based anti-seize, 0.30 for dry steel
where.F: Desired bolt tension (lbs) — typically 60-70% of bolt yield strength

bolt_torque_calculation.example: 1-inch B7 stud (yield = 105,000 psi, stress area = 0.606 sq in), target 60% yield: F = 0.60 x 105,000 x 0.606 = 38,178 lbs. With K = 0.20: T = 0.20 x 1.0 x 38,178 = 7,636 in-lbs = 636 ft-lbs. With anti-seize K = 0.15: T = 0.15 x 1.0 x 38,178 = 5,727 in-lbs = 477 ft-lbs.
bolt_torque_calculation.IMPORTANT: Nut factor K varies enormously with lubrication condition. ALWAYS use project-specified torque values when provided. This calculation is for reference/verification only.

thermal_expansion.formula: Delta_L = alpha x L x Delta_T

where.alpha: Coefficient of thermal expansion (inches per inch per degree F)

coefficients.carbon_steel: 6.5 x 10^-6 in/in/F (or approximately 0.78 inches per 100 feet per 100F)
coefficients.stainless_steel_304_316: 9.6 x 10^-6 in/in/F (approximately 1.15 inches per 100 feet per 100F)
coefficients.chrome_moly_2_25Cr: 7.1 x 10^-6 in/in/F (approximately 0.85 inches per 100 feet per 100F)

practical_use: Determines pipe guide and anchor spacing
Determines cold-spring offset for equipment nozzle alignment

fitting_takeout_dimensions.explanation: When calculating cut length, subtract fitting takeout from center-to-center dimension. Takeout is measured from fitting center (or end of fitting face) to the weld end.

90_degree_long_radius_elbow.formula: Takeout = 1.5 x Nominal Pipe Size (for standard long-radius elbows)

fitting_takeout_dimensions.tee_run: Takeout from center of tee to each run end. Equals fitting catalog dimension.
fitting_takeout_dimensions.tee_branch: Takeout from center of tee to branch end.

weld_shrinkage_allowance.butt_weld: Allow 1/16 to 1/8 inch per weld for shrinkage during welding
weld_shrinkage_allowance.note: Add this to cut length — weld shrinks the overall pipe length slightly

pipe_takeoff_basics.cut_length_formula: Cut Length = Center-to-Center dimension - Fitting Takeout(s) - Root Gap(s) + Weld Shrinkage Allowance(s)

pipe_weight_per_foot_CS.formula: Weight (lbs/ft) = 10.69 x (OD - wall thickness) x wall thickness
pipe_weight_per_foot_CS.for_stainless: Multiply carbon steel weight by 1.015 (stainless is about 1.5% heavier — negligible difference for field work)
pipe_weight_per_foot_CS.water_filled_add: Add water weight = 0.3405 x ID^2 (lbs per foot, where ID in inches)
